worth_it_explanation: Competitive separation and roadblocks are contractual promises a publisher makes to direct-sold advertisers, so a gap is a breach the advertiser can see on the live page. The audit is quick because both controls live in the Ad Manager UI and can be confirmed on a test page. Competitive exclusion labels are inspected under Inventory then Labels and traced to the line items that should carry them. Roadblock delivery is read from the takeover line items and verified by loading a page. Catching a missing label or a misconfigured roadblock before the advertiser does protects the relationship and the revenue tied to it.
intro: In Google Ad Manager (GAM), competitive exclusion labels keep rival advertisers off the same page, and roadblocks deliver one advertiser across every slot on a page. Both are commitments a publisher makes when it sells inventory directly, so a configuration gap reads as a broken promise to the advertiser. These checks confirm that competing advertisers carry matching exclusion labels and that takeover deals are set up to deliver as full roadblocks.

audit_checks.title: Competitive exclusion labels are missing or incomplete
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When exclusion labels are not applied to the line items that need separation, two competing advertisers can serve on the same page, which breaks the separation a direct deal promised. The advertiser sees a rival next to its own ad, and the publisher carries the breach risk on a deal it already booked.

educate.base:
A competitive exclusion label in Google Ad Manager (GAM) is a label you create once and then apply to line items so that two line items carrying the same label do not deliver together on the same page. A publisher uses it to honor the separation a direct-sold advertiser expects. Two car brands assigned the same 'Auto' label will not appear side by side, which is the experience the advertisers contracted for.

The separation only happens when the label is actually applied to every line item that should carry it. A label that exists in the account but sits unapplied, or that is applied to one competitor's line items and not the other's, leaves the advertisers eligible to serve together. The promise reads as kept in the account, yet the page can still show both.

One detail shapes how far the separation reaches. Competitive exclusion holds within a single ad request, so the publisher's tags need to send the page's slots together as a single request architecture (SRA) call. When slots are requested separately, GAM evaluates each request on its own and cannot guarantee the exclusion across them. A publisher that wants reliable separation may want to confirm the page uses SRA, not only that the label is applied.

If two advertisers from the same advertiser should be allowed to serve together despite a shared label, GAM offers a same advertiser exception you can enable on the label so the publisher's own multi-line-item campaigns are not held apart by mistake.

A few signs point to exclusion labels being missing or incomplete:
  • A label exists under Labels but is not applied to any line item
  • One competitor's line items carry the label and the rival's do not
  • The page requests its ad slots separately rather than as a single request
You may want to trace each advertiser that needs separation back to its line items and confirm the label set is complete before deciding the control is working.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, the advertisers that need separation are usually the endemic categories you sell against, such as competing automakers, airlines, or banks. You may want to start the audit from the category groups you promise separation on and confirm every line item in each group carries the matching label, rather than checking a single flagship deal and assuming the rest follow.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content publisher, the products you review can put competing retailers or brands on the same page. You may want to confirm that exclusion labels cover the review and category templates where two rival brands are most likely to be eligible at once, not only the home page.

investigate.intro: Confirm that every advertiser needing competitive separation carries a complete, applied exclusion label set, and that the pages requesting the inventory send their slots as a single request.

gam_ui.label: Google Ad Manager UI
steps: Open 'Inventory', then 'Labels', and list the competitive exclusion labels that exist in the account, for example 'Auto' or 'Airlines'.
For each label, open the line items that should carry it and confirm the label is applied to every competitor in that category, not only to one advertiser.
Note any label that exists but is applied to no line items, and any category where one competitor carries the label and the rival does not.
If the publisher's own campaigns should be allowed to serve together despite a shared label, confirm the same advertiser exception is enabled on that label.

google_publisher_console.label: Google Publisher Console
steps: Load a page where two competing advertisers could be eligible at once and open the Google Publisher Console.
Inspect the page requests and confirm the slots are sent together as a single request rather than as separate requests. Competitive exclusion holds only within one request.
Record whether the page uses single request architecture. A separated request can let labeled competitors serve together even when the labels are correct.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per advertiser category that needs separation, whether the exclusion label exists, whether it is applied to every competing advertiser's line items, and whether the serving pages use a single request. A category passes when the label is applied to every relevant line item and the page sends its slots as a single request. A category where the label is unapplied, applied to only one competitor, or undermined by separated requests is a fail recorded with the category name and the gap found.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the label status and the request observation for each advertiser category that needs separation above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that competitive exclusion labels on {{site_name}} are missing or incomplete for advertisers that were promised separation. Without the label applied to every competing line item, two rival advertisers can serve on the same page, which breaks the separation the direct deal committed to and exposes the publisher to a breach the advertiser can see. {{voice_recommend}} creating the exclusion label for each category that needs separation, applying it to every competing advertiser's line items, and enabling the same advertiser exception where the publisher's own campaigns should still serve together. {{voice_recommend}} also confirming the serving pages use a single request. Competitive exclusion holds only within one ad request.

audit_checks.title: Roadblocks are not configured for sponsorship takeovers
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A paid takeover that should fill every slot with one advertiser instead renders partially or mixes with other demand when the roadblock is not set up to deliver as guaranteed. The advertiser paid for a full-page presence and receives a fractional one, which is a delivery shortfall on a premium deal.

educate.base:
A roadblock in Google Ad Manager (GAM) delivers multiple creatives from a single line item together on the same page, which is how a publisher renders a sponsorship takeover where one advertiser owns every slot. A guaranteed roadblock is the version that commits to serving all of the creatives together, and it is what a paid takeover deal relies on.

A guaranteed roadblock has two requirements. The line item's roadblock setting has to be 'All', so GAM delivers only when there is room for every creative at once. The page's tags also have to use single request architecture (SRA), so GAM learns about all of the slots in one request and can fill them as a set. When SRA is not in place, GAM treats the line item as 'As many as possible' and the takeover is no longer guaranteed, so it can render in some slots and not others.

The same advertiser context matters alongside the roadblock. A takeover means one advertiser across the whole page, so any competitive exclusion labels on that advertiser need the same advertiser exception enabled, otherwise the advertiser's own creatives can hold each other apart. The roadblock setting controls whether the creatives serve together, and the labels control whether the advertiser is allowed to repeat across the page.

A takeover that is booked but not configured this way reads as live in the account while the page tells a different story. You may want to treat a partial or mixed takeover as a delivery setup to trace through the roadblock setting and the page's request mode rather than a one-off render glitch.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, takeovers are often sold against a high-traffic section front or a tentpole event page, so the roadblock has to be correct on the exact pages the advertiser bought, not the site as a whole. You may want to test a representative page in each sold placement and confirm every slot fills with the takeover advertiser before the campaign goes live.

investigate.intro: Review the takeover line items for the guaranteed roadblock settings, confirm the serving pages use a single request, and load a page to verify the takeover fills every slot.

gam_ui.label: Google Ad Manager UI
steps: Open the takeover line item and confirm its roadblock delivery is set to 'All' rather than 'As many as possible'. Only 'All' guarantees the full takeover.
Confirm guaranteed roadblocks are enabled for the network, which an administrator sets. The line item's 'All' setting depends on it.
If the takeover advertiser carries competitive exclusion labels, confirm the same advertiser exception is enabled so the advertiser's own creatives are not held apart on the page.

google_publisher_console.label: Google Publisher Console
steps: Load a page in the sold placement and open the Google Publisher Console to inspect the ad requests.
Confirm the page sends its slots as a single request. A guaranteed roadblock falls back to 'As many as possible' without single request architecture.
Check every slot on the rendered page and record whether the takeover advertiser fills all of them or only some.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per takeover deal, the roadblock delivery setting, whether guaranteed roadblocks are enabled for the network, whether the serving page uses a single request, and what the test page rendered. A takeover passes when the line item is set to 'All', the network allows guaranteed roadblocks, the page uses a single request, and every slot on the test page fills with the takeover advertiser. A takeover that renders partially, mixes with other demand, or runs without single request architecture is a fail recorded with the deal name and the setting at fault.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the roadblock setting, the request mode, and the test page result for each takeover deal above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that sponsorship takeovers on {{site_name}} are not configured to deliver as guaranteed roadblocks. A takeover that should fill every slot with one advertiser instead renders partially or mixes with other demand, so the advertiser receives a fractional version of the full-page presence it paid for. {{voice_recommend}} setting the takeover line item's roadblock delivery to 'All', confirming guaranteed roadblocks are enabled for the network, and confirming the serving pages use a single request so the creatives deliver as a set. {{voice_recommend}} also enabling the same advertiser exception on any competitive exclusion labels the takeover advertiser carries, then loading a test page to confirm every slot fills with the takeover.
